summary: O'Neil shares an article by journalism student Caroline Chen about the status of Mochizuki's ABC Conjecture proof, for which O'Neil was interviewed. While recommending the article, she takes issue with its title 'The Paradox of the Proof' because she dislikes the word 'paradox.' She explains her objection: calling something a paradox often means the speaker is avoiding examining their own assumptions. The post is brief, serving mainly to promote Chen's work while noting personal connections to people featured in the article.
key_points: O'Neil was interviewed about Mochizuki's ABC Conjecture proof status, following her earlier skeptical post on the subject
She recommends Chen's article despite disagreeing with its title
Her critique of the word 'paradox' reveals a methodological principle: paradoxes often indicate unexamined assumptions
The post highlights O'Neil's connections to the mathematical community through friends and her husband who appear in the article
money_quotes: When someone calls something a paradox, it means they are making an assumption that they don't want to examine.
key_insight: What we call a 'paradox' is often just an unexamined assumption in disguise.
